title: Programmable dipolar interaction geometry selects stripe-family order in a molecular lattice quantum simulator
abstract: Microwave-dressed polar molecules offer a route to lattice quantum simulators in which the angular form of long-range dipolar interactions, not only their overall strength, can be engineered. We study this setting in a minimal hard-core Bose lattice model on a square optical lattice, with particles interacting through a sign-changing non-axisymmetric dipolar tail \mathcal V(\mathbf r)\propto (x^2-y^2)/(x^2+y^2)^{5/2} that is repulsive along one lattice axis and attractive along the other. Using worm-algorithm path-integral quantum Monte Carlo simulations, supported by a hard-core spin mapping and a Gutzwiller soft-mode diagnostic, we find two regimes controlled by t/V: at larger t/V the system remains superfluid but develops a pronounced directional stiffness anisotropy, while at smaller t/V it forms a stripe solid selected in the (q,0) axial family, corresponding to real-space stripes parallel to y. The leading ordering wave vector remains in this axial family but reorganizes with filling, showing that the robust ordered object is a family of stripe states rather than one fixed commensurate Bragg peak. Near the closure of the stripe lobe, averaged observables can mimic a narrow supersolid signal; measurement-resolved stripe structure-factor histograms instead reveal first-order switching between superfluid and stripe-solid sectors. NaCs lattice estimates place the relevant V/t window within reach of modest effective dressed dipole moments, linking the predicted stripe-family order and its experimental diagnostics to accessible molecular quantum-simulation scales.
summary: This paper studies how anisotropic, sign-changing dipolar interactions in a molecular lattice simulator drive the formation of a stripe-family ordered phase. It shows that the interaction geometry selects specific axial stripes and that the transition to this phase is a first-order process. The findings are linked to realistic experimental scales for NaCs polar molecules.
main_problem: Investigating how programmable, non-axisymmetric, and sign-changing dipolar interaction geometries select specific density-ordering patterns (stripe-family order) in a molecular lattice quantum simulator.
main_result: The interaction geometry selects an axial (q,0) stripe-family where the wave vector evolves with filling, and the transition from superfluid to stripe-solid is identified as a first-order switching rather than a stable supersolid phase.
method: Worm-algorithm path-integral quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) simulations, supported by a hard-core spin mapping and a Gutzwiller soft-mode diagnostic.
model_or_system: A 2D square optical lattice of microwave-dressed polar molecules (specifically NaCs) modeled as a minimal hard-core Bose-Hubbard model with a non-axisymmetric, sign-changing dipolar tail.
key_observables: Directional superfluid stiffness anisotropy, structure factor S(q), compressibility, and measurement-resolved structure-factor histograms.
important_parameters: Ratio of hopping to interaction strength (t/V), chemical potential (mu), and lattice filling (n).
main_assumptions: The use of a strict hard-core constraint (infinite onsite repulsion) and the deep-lattice/point-particle approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the lattice interaction geometry; Figure 2 presents the ground-state phase diagram; Figure 7 shows Gutzwiller diagnostics for stripe period; Figure 8 shows real-space density maps.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the programmable interaction geometry, presents the numerical phase diagram and phase transition analysis, provides a Gutzwiller-based mechanism for stripe selection, and concludes with an experimental feasibility study for NaCs molecules.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how engineering the angular dependence of long-range interactions can be used as a primary tool to program complex many-body phases and density-wave patterns in programmable quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Model and interaction geometry. (a) Hard-core bosons hop with amplitude t on a square lattice and interact through a long-range, sign-changing density interaction Vij = V(rij). Red and blue links indicate the repulsive and attractive axial sectors, while the dashed diagonals mark the nodal directions. (b) Angular part of the interaction, proportional to cos 2ϕ, illustrating its dx2−y2-like sign structure. (c) Representative real-space interaction window computed directly from Eq. (2); a sign-preserving compressed color scale is used for visibility, with warm and cool colors denoting positive and negative matrix elements, respectively. (d) Representative dense-grid momentum-space...

FIG. 2. Ground-state phase diagram. (a) Ground state phase diagram in the (t/V, µ/V ) plane. Blue and pink denote the superfluid and stripe-solid regimes, respectively; the solid black boundary is bracketed from the combined compressibility, superfluid-stiffness, and stripe-family diagnostics. Dashed curves indicate the crossover toward the trivial empty and full limits. (b) The same boundary replotted in the (n, V/t) plane, highlighting the particle-hole-related structure about half filling. The stripe lobe first appears near n = 1/2, broadens with increasing V/t, and closes near V/t = 2.18, as analyzed in Fig. 3.

FIG. 3. First-order closure of the stripe-solid lobe at t/V ≃0.459. (a) L = 24 parameter sequence near half filling: the left axis gives the superfluid stiffness ρs, while the right axis gives the stripe-family structure factor S⋆ x. (b) Measurement-resolved normalized distribution of the fixed stripe structure factor Sℓ(π, 0) at µ = 0; the horizontal axis is the unscaled Sℓ(π, 0) value. The simultaneous dip in the averaged winding response, growth of the stripe structure fac- tor, and two-sector structure of this distribution indicate first- order switching near the lobe closure rather than a separate stable supersolid phase.

FIG. 4. System-size dependence of a representative stripe-solid boundary. Representative transition region at t/V ≃0.385 for L = 12, 24, 36, plotted as a function of µ/V . (a) Superfluid stiffness ρs; (b) stripe-family structure factor S⋆ x. The L = 12 response is broadened over several points, whereas the L = 24 and L = 36 curves show a much sharper jump, consistent with first-order behavior. Error bars denote statistical uncertainties; the drift of the jump position with L gives the finite-size uncertainty used when assigning the phase boundary in Fig. 2.

FIG. 5. Anisotropic superfluid response at larger hop- ping ratio, t/V = 0.5. Representative t/V = 0.5 case for L = 24, plotted against filling n, where hopping is relatively stronger than in the stripe-ordered regime and the system re- mains superfluid throughout the compressible window. (a) Directional superfluid stiffnesses ρs,x, ρs,y, and their average ρs. The superfluid response is finite over a broad interval and vanishes only near the trivial empty and full limits. A clear directional imbalance, ρs,x > ρs,y, persists across most of the superfluid regime. (b) Stiffness anisotropy factor Aρ. It remains finite inside the superfluid window and decreases toward zero near the density...
